text.title: SLO Burn Matrix — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every burn-decision matrix must satisfy. The rules ensure burn alerts produce action, not debate.

rule.statement: The matrix uses EXPLICIT burn categories (fast-burn 1h window, fast-burn 6h window, slow-burn 24h, slow-burn 72h, near-exhaustion) AND service classes (user-facing critical, user-facing non-critical, async worker, batch, internal tool). At least 3 categories × 3 classes minimum.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook multi-window multi-burn-rate; field experience

rule.rationale: A two-cell matrix (fast vs slow) cannot capture the difference between a 1-hour blip and a sustained 3-day burn. The 3×3 floor produces meaningful action differentiation; sparser matrices collapse to "page someone".

rule.statement: Each cell maps to a specific action from the enumerated set: `halt_canary`, `freeze_ship`, `focus_shift`, `accept`, `investigate`. Empty cells or "see PM" are forbidden.
rule.rationale: An undefined cell becomes a live debate when the alert fires. Pre-decided actions remove that overhead. The named enum prevents inventive ad-hoc actions during incident pressure.

rule.statement: Each action has a named owner: the role that executes the action (e.g. `oncall_primary`, `release_manager`, `tech_lead`). Group owners ("the team") are forbidden.
rule.rationale: When the action is `halt_canary` and the action message lands in a shared channel, "the team" diffuses responsibility. The named role pulls the action — the person occupying that role at the time acts.

rule.statement: Each action has explicit revert criteria: what must be true for the action to lift (e.g. ship freeze lifts when burn drops below slow-burn threshold for 24h AND postmortem actions tracked). Open-ended actions are forbidden.
rule.rationale: A ship freeze without revert criteria becomes a permanent culture; releases stop, then resume informally without trust. Explicit revert criteria make the action a finite project.

rule.statement: Every matrix-triggered action is written to an immutable audit log: alert id, matrix cell, action taken, owner, start time, revert time, notes. The log feeds the weekly burn review.
rule.rationale: Without an audit log, the matrix's effectiveness cannot be assessed. The log also defends the team when leadership asks "why did you freeze ships last Friday?" — the matrix entry, the alert, the named action.

section.title: Sample 3x3 matrix
section.code: | fast-burn (1h) | fast-burn (6h) | slow-burn (72h) -----------------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------ user-facing critical | halt_canary | halt_canary + | freeze_ship + | | freeze_ship | focus_shift user-facing non-crit | investigate | halt_canary | focus_shift async worker | investigate | investigate | accept (note in log)

example.title: Fast-burn on user-facing critical service
example.code: 14:22 — burn alert fires: user-facing critical service consuming 2h-window budget at 14x normal rate. 14:22 — matrix lookup: halt_canary action. 14:22 — alert manager pages oncall_primary AND release_manager. 14:24 — release manager halts the in-progress canary deploy. 14:25 — investigation begins; root cause: a config error in the latest deploy. 14:50 — rollback completes; burn rate returns to baseline. 15:10 — revert criteria met (rate < threshold for 30 min); action closed. Audit log: cell (fast-1h, user-facing critical) → halt_canary → success.

example.title: No matrix; burn debated in real-time
example.code: 14:22 — burn alert fires. Slack thread: "is this a big deal? should we freeze deploys? what about the marketing launch tomorrow?" 45-minute discussion. Decision: "let us watch it." By 17:00, burn has consumed 60% of the monthly budget.
example.note: r2 violated (no action), r3 violated (no owner). The team debates instead of acting.
